item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
upvoted before i even read it, i'm just so happy this was brought back in any way shape or form! :D

without this, the extended page of SCP-2305 would have been missing one of its more epic pieces.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes:
http://www.scp-wiki.net/2117-recovered-materials

There's two links to this in the article….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This brings some nostalgic feelings to my inner surface.

Years ago I read an SCP similar to this. As far as I can remember, only one link was on that page. Peopl could make their own additions to the ship through that page.

What happend to that page? Feels like it got deleted but why? Is there a way to acces the old page via an archive or something similar?

Don't get me wrong, I enjoy this SCP too. It's just that the mutli-race spaceship with anomalous language translation that I know is one of my top 3 SCPs ever.

If it's gone then that's how it is. I just really miss the whole "make your addition to this SCP"-thing and I wish to at least see that part once again.

If I did not knew the old SCP, this would at the very least be a 8.5/10, which is really high since I can be quite picky. But with the nostlagic feeling of something missing- I dunno man. It just feels weird, really really weird.

Still props to the creator tho; very well written and the overview at the bottom makes scrolling through the pages really easy and a lot more fun and interesting.

Edit: I get it now. One question tho: can we add new parts to the ship somehow or is that feat gone?

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded:
I've genuinely never read the original. I came to this with a lot of skepticism (I'm not even familiar with the original 'canon' around these characters, so a lot of it was just leaping in blind).

To my surprise, I enjoyed this; it was well-written, and while some of it felt a tad bit self-indulgent, there was enough intriguing character-work to keep me engaged from beginning to end.

ETA: Pardon; I didn't mean to make this post a reply.

Need I say anything more?

Okay, fine. Thanks to everyone who's upvoted and commented on this in the last 300 days of its existence. It was a rough ride getting this written, and I honestly wish I didn't have to write it, but it's here, and it's staying.

 
 

Kimi wa kikoeru? Boku no ko no koe ga yami ni munashiku suikoma reta...

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had doubts at first, because I loved the original one, but honestly I'm glad to see the new one being fully integrated to the Stealing Solidarity canon. I miss the original log of recovered materials, of course, like a lot of other people in the comments, but what's done is done.

So yeah, it was a lot of work but it's now translated in French. Again. Because I still love it in this new version.

If 'Solidarity' is deleted again and I have to translate a third, even longer version, I will throw myself into the sea, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sometime, it is written SCP-2772. Is it simple mistake?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this so much. How does this not have over +100? This is so underrated.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Wholeheartedly approved. In fact, since ListPages and child pages can be a bit finicky, I'll add it in myself.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

Author has already made the edits discussed below. Timer removed.

+ show block
– hide block

Given that this is set in the "Stealing the Solidarity" tale series/canon, it may behoove new readers who are unfamiliar with that source material to have a link there. As such, I am suggesting the following:

Editing this:

SCP-2117-B instances are currently acting as security consultants with the Foundation as part of Auxiliary Task Force Alpha-1 ("The Black Rabbit In The Moon"). SCP-2117-B instances are allowed free passage on to and off of SCP-2117, and are being used as a disruptive agents for non-Foundation interests in China, Japan and South Korea.

To this:

SCP-2117-B instances are currently acting as security consultants with the Foundation as part of Auxiliary Task Force Alpha-1 ("The Black Rabbit In The Moon"). SCP-2117-B instances are allowed free passage on to and off of SCP-2117, and are being used as a disruptive agents for non-Foundation interests in China, Japan and South Korea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Congratulations! You found the point of the story! SCP-2085 are the main characters of this, celebrating the fact that the Solidarity is no longer in the possession of its original author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad I'm not alone. I thought the concept of this SCP was really interesting, but then it turned into a story about cyborg catgirls. It felt incredibly forced.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really good, but I do have a big question. Why the whole thing with additional ships being added to the original? I didn't really see any reason for it, especially since everyone on the other ships (Space Mormons included) was already dead by the time the Foundation showed up, and didn't really affect the plot at all. The logs even acted like there was still only one of everything. And while the idea of a couple hundred Solidarities teaming up to destroy the Hateful Star was pretty cool, it didn't really seem to have anything to do with the rest of the SCiP.

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: Mark Penrice, instead of replying to your own comments, please edit your previous post using the "edit" function under the "options" tab to the lower right of every comment. That prevents spam buildup.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*checks out the sandbox-backed-up "original version"*

…aaaaaand immediately those questions are on the one hand vaporised, on the other hand magnified. The original version had much more realistic dimensions (roughly Mento-shaped, about 25.2km diameter x 6km high), and a better grasp of spacial units (various items described as having kilometre-cubed sizes, memetic field AoA being a radius measured in linear kilometres), it would appear. Which begs the question: why muck around with them, when what you've got is already a very, very large spaceship/space station that could eat, e.g. DS9 or B5 for breakfast and consider them mere appetisers? Even the "32km to engine core" bit would very nearly have still fit (if you've got to go, say, 25km along and then 5km down, following a less than direct route…), but even if not, having the distance be 20km rather than 20 miles would still make it a damn long way to travel inside an artificial vessel.

(For reference, Rama was 50km long and 20km across, and that was an interstellar generation ship where only the inner surface of the cylinder was built upon, rather than having level, low-rise decks; Red Dwarf, which was essentially a spaceborn mining town of 3000+ people and ultimately itself capable of interstellar travel, measured about 6 x 4.5 x 3km and never really seemed crowded even with a full, living crew, even though we must assume a good portion of its interior was given over to engines, supplies storage, and moreover vast volumes for reception, processing, and storage of processed mined material… its job was to travel the solar system turning asteroids into saleable metals, after all…)

I mean, the rest of it, so far, no problem, it's just the dimensions thing that's bugging.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At the point of having read the landing page and most of the initial exploration log, I have to ask… did you really mean to make it "200km x 50km x 70km"? That's a humongous space, in the realm of the Imperial Death Star, way beyond almost any typical fictional space station or ship (or, heck, a droppable space colony) and certainly on the scale of various smaller moons; in fact, it would be something like the ~300th largest object in the entire solar system, and rating fairly high on the list of Things Orbiting Saturn - with its longest dimension equal to the diameter of Mimas, and total volume on a par with Epimetheus. Total mass would probably be enough to affect Titan's orbit in a small but measurable way.

Even if it's multiple (PEP-violating duplicates of) smaller ships mashed together, with less than 100 involved they must each have been individually monstrous in of themselves. And one wonders why you'd need TARDIS-like interior non-Euclidian space bending technology; if we assume a pretty generous 3.5m separation between each floor (say, a similar 2.5m ceiling to what my own apartment offers, plus 1m of floor thickness for bulkheads and services), with 70km being the "height" and 200x50km for the expanse of each deck, the total floor area is approximately 200 million square kilometres. The total land area on Earth is… a little under 150 million square kilometres.

+ show block
– hide block

The suggestion that the engines can produce a bubble of "upto 600 square kilometres" to envelop the ship and allow it to teleport also make me wonder if there's a misunderstanding of basic units in play. Never mind that it would have to be *cubic* kilometres to encompass a solid volume, that figure would be hopelessly short of the requirement for either volume *or* simple square footage when multiplying any of the straight line dimensions together - even 50x70km comes to 3500 sqkm. Or was it meant to be "600km diameter" (or radius)? Still, if you've got that kind of power, you don't really need to warp the space station to wherever a looming threat has appeared. Just aim the engines at one of the other Saturnalian moons (e.g. Enceladus would fit quite nicely) and warp *that* to the threat's location for a few seconds, then straight back to its normal orbit. The mother of all telefrags…

((edit: OK, the list I was looking at for reference to some of those above, I just realised lists radius not diameter. Which accounts for the massive size discrepancy between the first two moons, when they should be somewhat closer together. Volume is still similar to Epimetheus, but diameter vs longest edge is more like Janus or Phoebe. Still a major Saturn-orbiting object in its own right, though; however, it's fallen down the Solar System list to somewhere in the 400 or 500s (there are a LOT of things out there we now know about that are bigger than most of Saturn's moons). Does make it more realistic to have only been recently discovered (and that with the help of a distress beacon), though, given that IRL mundane astronomy has only been capable of reliably picking up sub-1000km diameter objects at Kuiper Belt distance over about the last 20 years, and initially only the spherical and more icy ones at that…))

((Hmm, thinking about it… Planet 10… is this written with the intent that Pluto is still Planet Nine? And so Eris is/was either Ten or Eleven? (There would be some validity to the idea, if one insists on finding fault with the IAU's somewhat shaky planetoid classification system - even though there are moons larger than both, and they've "failed to clear their orbit", they are a/ free floating, b/ by far and away the largest Dwarfs, yet almost exactly the same diameter despite a considerable difference in mass, and c/ have their own moons… And, heck, Pluto-Charon gets a whole lot larger, conceptually, if we consider it as a single binary system instead of a small planetoid with a very large moon…)


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

Or are we now missing a larger planet we didn't even know about, and yet to discover the actual Nine?))

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: (oops I missed the Hateful Star reference the first time)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I could not make it through. This screams anime fanfiction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sugi ni omae wa 'This log will end' to iu!

This log will end! Nani?

Fucking hell. As if I'm not tired enough of Part 2.

I miss the old skip. I wish to see the original recovered materials log in full. That was the prime source of entertainment.

item.title: Brilliant
item.content:encoded:
You cured the hole in my heart after seeing 2722 go, and concluded the fantastic series that is Stealing Solidarity in one fell swoop.

:D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked the old version better. 2722 was my absolute favourite SCP, and it is very sad to see it gone.

For all those who loved the original version as much as I did, I made a backup in a sandbox: http://scp-sandbox-3.wikidot.com/backup2722

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wonderful! It makes me immensely happy to see Solidarity firmly rooted in the tale series that made me fall in love with the ship in the first place, and it tugs at my heartstrings to see the BRC/Stealing Solidarity get a happy epilogue.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am…underwhelmed. I'm assuming the original iteration of this concept went bye-bye when the author decamped to SCP-XXX-POL, in which case deleting his articles was pretty petulant, but this is not possessed of the same charm as the original; in addition to which, whilst I liked the BRC scp itself, I'm opposed to binding the Solidarity to their canon in the mainlist.
Basically this feels like an anime-gimmick rewrite of a better article, I honestly think that Solidarity should be allowed to rest in peace and the BRC should move on, rather than resurrecting it into some uncanny valley version of itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For reasons that are best left undiscussed here, you’d probably have better luck asking on the SCP subreddit. I’m sure an archived copy exists somewhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry,but what happened to the original. Dose anyone have a link to the old one or an archive of it, I really want to see the how much it changed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-2722 was my favourite SCP on this site and i am so happy to see it wont just be deleted forever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a few reservations.

I was never too familiar with the canons involving Solidarity, but you should probably at least link the Stealing Solidarity Hub somewhere in this article.

I was left uncertain as to what caused the ship to go into disarray with the BRC still alive and no threat left to fight. I assume this was explained in the aforementioned hub, but I don't know. I was expecting an Aliens situation.

This new article feels like a prologue. I was hoping that the BRC and The Foundation would team up against some big bad in this. Maybe in the future, or is there a hub for this already (and if so can we have a link to it in this article)?

Finally… is this article… allowed? A loophole is being exploited here. On one hand I'm happy that the Solidarity is back, and on the other hand it feels disrespectful. Did Von Pincier allow this, or does his opinion not matter in this regard? Trudging into new territory I suppose…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We did a whole Super Sentai thing where we all yelled out the name of the attack at once. It was great.

Adorable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I could not possibly give this a stronger upvote. I am 100% for this. It's a great rewrite/reimaging of an old article that brings up some exciting new concepts while telling a refreshing story.

EDIT: Also, there should be an option to blow the picture up to full-size. You can see the full-size image by right-clicking, but with some very simple code, you can have it be done with a single click. Just a recommendation.

Code
– hide block

[[include component:image-block name=2117.png|caption=Former exterior plans of SCP-2117, retrieved from an onboard computer. More detailed mapping operations are currently underway. Click to enlarge.|link="http://scp-wiki.wdfiles.com/local--files/fragment:2117-1/2117.png"]]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I honestly can't support using the name of an author-deleted article.

item.title: Re: Ours Is An Author Commentary That Will Pierce The Heavens
item.content:encoded: I also thought this was too happy while reading it. But knowing that you were also aware of it while writing it? I think I'm kinda okay with it now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The link back to the core files (offset 0) is broken from all other offsets.

Aside from that… I like it. Upboated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Relation to the BRC is new, right? I like it. It’s a sensible linking of canon.

E: I wasn’t familiar with Stealing Solidarity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now this is a proper send-off. The circle is closed.

item.title: Ours Is An Author Commentary That Will Pierce The Heavens
item.content:encoded:
Mood Music: Yui- Again (OP 1 for Fullmetal Alchemist: Brotherhood)

So, this has a complicated history. It's based off of the original SCP-2722, which got pulled down for reasons that will not be discussed here. This left a rather large hole in the canon, and this needed to be rectified.

Djoric originally was going to take over the rewrite (and he eventually did) but I offered to take up the reigns after a while. The theme of this can be summed up by Omega-2's name: "Anti-Nihilism IN SPACE!". This is firmly set in Stealing Solidarity, after the Solidarity has been Stolen… for quite some time. The Black Rabbit Company has gone through trials and tribulations, and has gotten tired of adventuring, but are still, ultimately, a force for happiness. And god knows we need that nowadays.

I gotta give a lot of credit to Djoric for this, obviously, for letting me use his characters, and giving me a lot of ideas for this. The original ending was more climactic, but then he pointed out that they'd been adventuring for so long that it wouldn't be right to end it on an action-packed thing. They'd need a quiet life.

My biggest concern about this was the same that I have about a lot of my works: That it's too noblebright for a Foundation story. For those of you who don't know "noblebright" is the polar opposite of "GrimDark"— the original Star Wars trilogy is NobleBright, and Warhammer 40k is GrimDark. The majority of my tales are in this style, because why have angst and overwhelming sadness when you can have adventure and character development? But I'm afraid I may have overdone it in this. Ah, well.

As for what the impending apocalypse/status quo is that's implied in the last two documents… I'll leave it up to interpretation.

Special thanks to the following:

Gabriel Jade
undergroundmonorail
UraniumEmpire
stormbreath
Lazar Lyusternik
DrChandra
CharaNalaar
Lt Flops

The recovered materials log will be going up some time this week, can't promise when.

Image is originally by Dr Reach, modified for this article by Cyantreuse, under the Creative Commons license.

E: Just so I don't get accused of plagiarism (and I should have attributed this earlier, my bad) the description of SCP-2117-B instances is lifted pretty much wholesale from Djoric's SCP-2085. Additionally, some portions of text within the Extended Exploration Logs page (particularly the description of the plaque on the Bridge) was taken from the original SCP-2722.
